speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Lucille Riddell Denavent took place at her home in Lewiston on November 11, 1975.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first think you were interested in becoming a nurse?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, I always wanted to be a nurse and tell my little girl. We lived close to a hospital in Warren, Minnesota. My mother said they used to follow those nurses down the street. Every chance I got, I guess I admired their uniforms. That's what I liked. So I always wanted to be a nurse as long as I can remember I wanted to be a nurse. Well, I wanted to go to Minneapolis and take my training because I thought I'd have many more advantages, you know, bigger schools and everything. But my mother didn't want me to be a nurse. She wanted me to be a teacher. And I told her that I didn't think I was smart enough to be a teacher. See, I only had one year of high school. That's all I had. And she said, well, you can go on to school and be a teacher. She said, you know, nurses aren't pop much out. And so my older sister who was 17 years older than I was just like a mother.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: She said, well, why don't I take her?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Because see, I was only 16.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And why don't I take her with me and take care of her?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And she only lived two blocks from this small hospital. Well, I got very good training there. And then I had my last six months in Chicago, which is wonderful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You trained right at the hospital?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Yeah. That was at Mobridge, South Dakota, where my sister lived. And then my last six months I had at the Washington Boulevard Hospital in Chicago. So that was good. Then I came from there to Mobridge. Let me ask a couple of things about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what was the basis for your mother thinking that nurses were quite up to?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, I don't think that years ago that people fought too much of nurses. I really don't think so. You know, the idea of them having to bathe men and all that, you know, my mother didn't like that. She was a person that didn't go along with that at all, you know. And she thought that it would be so much nicer if I would be a teacher and that I would be looked up to so much more if I were a teacher and everything. She said another thing. It was awfully hard work, which it was. It was really hard work. And she just didn't want me to be a nurse. She thought that I could do better than being a nurse. But now look at the nurses, the wages the nurses make now. Of course, we didn't. And when I look back and think how we worked and really took care of the patients and now the nurses don't do a damn thing and get all that money and I can't understand it. You know, you go to a hospital now and if you have any kind of an operation, they expect you the next day to get up and take care of yourself. So this one nurse said to me, well, it's better for you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, okay, if it's better for you, then why don't they let the patients go home instead of keeping them in the hospital? If you're going to take care of yourself, why can't you go home then? Why should you run up that big bill?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And, you know, those patients that have had these, you know, abdominal surgery, you know, major operations, they're up the next day taking care of themselves, going to the bathroom and taking their own baths and everything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, what if they faint or something like that?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: You know, nobody look after them. Those nurses don't pay any attention to them. Your mother, though, she changed her mind and let you go. So she wasn't really. Yes, but she didn't really want me to, but she did let me go because I was going with my sister who was 17 years older than I. And she put me through training and bought all my clothes and everything. I was about the best nurse nurse there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who, your sister or your mother?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: No, my sister. No, my mother didn't want anything to do with it. She didn't stop me from going, but she certainly wasn't happy about me going.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your sister doing?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: She was able to make. Well, she was married and had a husband with money. She helped support you then. She put me through my three years of training, bought all my clothes and I had anything I wanted. Because she never had any kids of her own. See, so I really was like her daughter to her, you know, and her husband was like me real well and anything that she wanted to do for me. Why that was fine with him. I know one time I saw a hat down town that I wanted and it was twelve dollars and a half. And I just mentioned it. That's all I did was just mention how pretty it was, you know, but I'm very long, couple days and I had my hat. So she had a really lovely home and she was a person that entertained a lot and everything. And instead of entertaining couples and everything like that, her husband had a clothing store. She would have these parties like on Thanksgiving and Christmas and New Year's for the young men around town that didn't have homes. And she fixed these big dinners for them, you know, have her table stretched out in her dining room. Gosh, I don't know how many she could set out of it. And most all of the time, if I was available so I could help her, I did. And if if I couldn't help her, why most of the time she did it herself. Just she and her husband. She do all that cooking for all those people. Wonderful cook, marvelous cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the young men doing? Was there a lot of railroad men?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Uh huh. So she was really doing that as a kindness. Yeah. Oh, yes, absolutely. Mm hmm. Now that big base there, my brother -in -law won that at a carnival and I had he gave me two of them and my sister had them packed in different boxes up in her garage. And of course, those days nobody thought about in their garage or their houses or anything. And then all my nurses books were in a in a box there too. And I just left them when I went to Chicago and I told her that probably I would need maybe a book or so. But I had taken what I thought I needed. Well, I needed a book and I sent her for it. So she went out in the garage to look for it. Here all my nurses book, somebody had stolen them and stole one of the boxes with the vase in it. So I only had the one, see. And I didn't have it for a long time because there was no way to get it to me. But finally I got to Mobridge and was able to bring it back with me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did uh, what was the training like for you when you were learning how to be a nurse in those days?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, I suppose it was probably like it is now. See, we had three months probation period. And now I think it's six months. I don't know. We only had three months. And of course, the first three months why we did mostly all the dirty work that nobody else wants to do. Why we did. And then when we had been there three months, we got our caps and we got our cuffs to our uniform and we got different aprons and different uniforms. We've got the striped uniforms then. And before we just had plain blue uniforms, you know, white aprons. And we had to furnish them ourselves. But the hospital gave us our uniforms and caps and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the how did you learn? Did you learn from the older nurses? Was that how?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, no, we had instructions. We had teachers and everything. We went to class every day and we had all kinds of books. Our anatomy was probably that thick a book. And we had to study just like going to school. You studied in at the same time you worked in the hospital. Oh, yeah, sure. We did. We had classes every day. Of course, not in the summertime, but we did all through the school term. We did just like any other anybody else, you know. And we had doctors and we had our head nurse. And it was Mobridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Consider was it actually a college called was it called a college?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, no, it was just a small hospital, just a very small hospital itself. Yeah. We had a couple of doctors over there and the second doctor. Why he taught a lot of our classes and our superintendent taught the classes too. Of course, I don't suppose we were probably as well educated. We were more doing things instead of being so book smart. You know, why we was more practical work with us, you know. But of course, we had to we had to take a state board and everything like that. So we had to know something about it, you know. Did become naturally easily. Oh, yeah, I loved every bit of it. And I would love to go back and do it over again. I've heard a lot of nurses say not me. And I said, well, I enjoyed every bit of it. And it's always been awful hard for me to understand how people can work and not be happy with their jobs. I just don't see how you could because I would think that you'd have to like your work to be happy and to make everybody else happy that you are taken care of and everything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, if you hated your work, well, how could you be a very kind nurse?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I couldn't I can't conceive of anything like that. But I've heard a lot of people say, oh, I just have to drive myself to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, well, Jeepers, why don't you get a different job then?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: You're not in the right thing. And I that's one thing I do think about the kids nowadays. They just don't know what they want to do. And I think in our time, we all of us pretty much knew, of course, we were all striving to get ahead and everybody was. See, for now, if you are striving to get ahead, you can usually make a pretty good goal of it because there's too many people that don't want to. They don't want to make anything of their lives, I guess. I don't know what they want to do. But I knew all my life what I wanted to do. And I like I say, I never was sorry for it. When you say when you say those days, everybody was striving to get ahead. Sure. That made.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that make it more difficult?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Sure. You better did. You better did because everybody was trying as hard as you were. See, and where now I think that if you really want to get ahead, you wouldn't have so much trouble because there's too many people that don't care about what they do or if they do anything or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But do you think that many people in those days were doing the kind of work they really wanted to do?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I think so because I think they knew what they wanted to do. I really do. And my older sister, who was seven years older than I, she wanted to be a music teacher. She was very talented. She played the piano beautifully. And my mother sent her to the conservatory in Minneapolis and it cost her $6 a lesson then. And that's years ago. It was probably about in 19. Oh, 16 and 17. And that girl practiced six hours a day so that she could get everything because she realized it was hard for my mother to pay that much for her and my younger sister, she really didn't want to do anything. And she never did do anything when she got out of high school. She made high school in three years. In fact, she was the only one in our family that graduated from high school. And there were seven of course, my older brother, my mother's first baby died when he was just nine months old. But the rest of us all went to high school, but none of us finished except Florence and she made it in three years, but she got married. She didn't do anything with her education at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The girl who went to the conservatory, did she, was she able to use that?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh my Lord. Yes. She played in church. She played for the shows and she taught music lessons and she did everything. And then when she got married, she happened to get a man that had a lot of money. She didn't get married young. She waited till she was 28 years old and he bought her a baby grand piano. This is the sister you stayed stayed with. Nope. Oh, this is now a younger, younger one. But my older sister was a school teacher and that's why mother thought that I should be a school teacher. See, she was teaching school out of the eighth grade. But your older sister, her idea was you should do what you wanted to. Yeah, absolutely. And that's what she, she talked to mother and she said, Oh, you're going to make a terrible mistake if you don't let Lucille do what she wants to do, because all her life she's wanted to be a nurse and she's crazy about the hospital and the nurses and the uniforms and the whole business. She said she's, you couldn't take her out of that and give her anything else to do because she wouldn't be happy. And I don't think I would have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It seems though that that an awful lot of women did, uh, of course, most of them didn't become either nurses or teachers.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I mean, most, uh, would get married, get married, raise a family. Of course they, um, uh, no, my mother wasn't married too young. She was 18 when she was married, but she said a lot of girls that she knew got married at 15 and 16 years old, you know, never had any high school. They were lucky if they had the eighth grade, you know, but I came along a little bit later than that. And, uh, I always wanted to get married as far as that goes, but I certainly wanted to be able to have something to, to do. Of course, my mother always told us, my dad died when I was 10 years old. My mother never remarried. And my mother always told us now, she said, I'll put you through high school. But when you get through high school, now you're on your own. You've got to make a way for yourself. See, and decide what you want to do and what you want to be. So in other words, uh, you in your, in your family, there was more, maybe more of a stress on, on, you know, I don't know if more so in my family than any other family, so, because they pretty much, uh, everybody went along the same line and their kids, you know, were all smart kids and they went away to school and everything and all made something out of themselves, you know, mostly teachers. Even the, uh, I went with the kid, I went through with them all through school and he wanted to be a doctor. And I always said, well, I wanted to be a nurse. And I said, well, when we get through why I really didn't think about marrying him, but I said, maybe I can come and work for you. He said, you bet you can come and work for me. But of course, I never was never happened to be where he was. But he was about three years older than I. And, uh, but we just knew that that's what we wanted to do. And he had to leave college. I don't know whether he was out one year or two years to make enough money so he could go on to finish college and go to medical school, you know, because in those days, our parents just didn't have that kind of money, you know, like people now they could, their kids can do pretty much what they want to do as far as money's concerned, but in those days, it wasn't so easy. And that's why I say the kids all had to work and make their own way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you went to Chicago for the last part of your training, was that the first time you'd gone to a really big city like that?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, I'll say it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did, what did the, how, what did Chicago seem like to you after being growing up in the rural?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, I don't know. I was so cock -eyed, busy when I was there that I really didn't have much time to really go an awful lot and I didn't go a lot because if you didn't have an escort, you just didn't go out in Chicago. That's all there is to it. And they just simply told us, you know, that we had to be awful careful, you know. So of course, when we, we did have dates, well, that was all right. But if we didn't have a date, why we used to go over on a Michigan Avenue where there was a Chinese restaurant and we spent all of our money over there. That's what we did. And that was a lot of fun, but we'd go with a bunch of girls, you know. And then when I did graduate, you know, this superintendent, she said, well, Mr. Dale, I certainly am surprised at you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, why?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I was always a little bit scared of her. I don't know. I was always a little bit on my guard, not that I didn't do my work and everything, but I just was a little bit leery of her and I was awful afraid of the head doctor too. And I met him one time in the hallway and he said, Mr. Dale, I want to ask you something. And I said, all right. And he said, every time I meet you, you act like you're scared of that for me. And I said, I am.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, why are you scared of me?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And I said, Oh, I don't know. I said, you just scare me. You're so gruff and everything. And he laughed. And so one time after I'd gotten out of surgery and I was working on the fourth floor, they called down for Mr. Dale to come up with surgery to help out. They were having a brain operation and quite a mess up there and everything. So as soon as I came in the door, he said to me, Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dale, do you know how to wipe up blood?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And I said, I sure do. Well, okay. Then he said, get busy around here and clean up this mess for us. But that's kind of a man he was, you know, but then after that, we got to be real good friends. And he told me, he felt awfully bad when I was going to leave because he said, you are a good nurse. And he said, we'd like to keep you here. And I said, well, you were awful slow asking me. And that was what I told Ms. Buckley, our superintendent. I said, well, I didn't know that you were interested in me. Well, she said, if I would have had any idea that you were planning on leaving, I would have asked you to stay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, why would you think that I wouldn't be looking for something to do when you didn't mention it to me?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I said, I thought probably that you weren't satisfied with my work. Oh, yes. She said, very satisfied with it. She said, we just, oh, hate to lose you. And I said, well, I'm sorry, but I've made other arrangements. Now there's nothing I can do about it. I said, I might come back, don't work for you sometime. And I did go back to Chicago, but I never went back to Washington Boulevard. I, my friend and I went to contagious. We worked out contagious in Chicago where there were nothing but children. And once in a while we'd have adults, but not very, not very often. It was scarlet fever and diphtheria, you know, and, uh, we worked out there for a long time. It was just like, uh, staying in a big hotel. Our dining room was just massive and all we had the most wonderful meals. They just treated us royal because it was, of course, it was hard to get nurses to work in those contagious places. Some of them were afraid, some didn't want to, and it was a county institution, you know, and, and, but you know how kids are all kids are nice. And I just loved it there too, but I did, I always liked my work and, uh, I don't know, like I love my home and I love to work in my home, you know, and it's hard for me to understand women that, uh, you know, really can't do anything and do a good job of it. I can't understand why that there wouldn't be one thing that they could, you know, do it and do it good, but you'd be surprised. They can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked, um, at Washington Boulevard, you were still in training, right?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah. Just the last part of your work. So you were likely to be assigned.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I was senior nurse, of course. Old. Sure. We were senior nurses in our last year, you know, we had a little bit of preference and not only that, when I went to Chicago, we got $15 a month. And that was just about like, uh, a hundred dollars a month now would be, you know, because that was a lot of money because we got our board and room and everything, you know, and of course I got money from home all the time. And, uh, so I just was living the life of Riley. That was all there was to it. Was there, we worked awfully hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a, uh, uh, uh, when you, when you say work awfully hard, how much, how much, uh, and, and what kind of work?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, we worked 10 hours a day. And, you know, we worked hard too. We really worked hard. We had a lot of patients to look after. They had to be bathed and taken care of and their beds had to be made. And brother, now I'm telling you, we had no coffee breaks or nothing like that. We worked and we worked our full 10 hours too, but at night duty, though, we got, we got two hours off at night there. And of course I didn't, when I was in Mowbridge, we worked straight 12 hours at night, but when we went to Chicago, you got two hours off at night. And sometimes, you know, if we weren't too busy, it was really nice because maybe you'd get five to seven off in the morning. Well, you could go home and sleep for a little while and then you could go out and have a good time the rest of the time.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, uh, was there a real, the difference between a nurse in training and a full fledged nurse, is there a lot of difference in the work that, uh, that the two of them, well, of course it would depend on what you were doing. Now, a lot of nurses, they, after they get through training, they go into doctor's offices or they like to be head nurses or, or they like to be special nurses or maybe they want to work in surgery, surgical nurses or something like that. That's what I wanted to be was a surgical nurse. And I wanted to go to Cook County in Chicago and take a six months course. But I felt like I should work a while, see, and that was bad for me. I should have gone right from training to Cook County and taken my six months. And then I could have had a real good job. Of course, I never really had much trouble getting a job. I was always able to, to work. I never had any trouble like that because of course the nurses are, we're always in demand then and they are now and always will be probably, as long as they have people in a way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, did you, uh, live with the other nurses in training? Was it like a dormitory?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And my sister, she'd call me up, you know, and she'd say, what hours you're going to have off?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And I'd say, oh, maybe one to three, two to four or something. Come down. I've got something for you. Now I'd go down there and maybe she'd bake the cake or maybe she had sandwiches made for me or something like that. She's always afraid of us from getting up to eat and Lord, they fed us some good. Cause I didn't have a chance to get fat because of work too hard to get fast. Living in Chicago too. No, no, that was in Mowbridge. Oh, she's always in Mowbridge. Yeah. She lived two blocks from the hospital. Oh no. When I went to Chicago, I didn't know anybody. Uh -oh, nobody. But of course I got in with the girls and it was a hundred bed hospital and there were quite a few nurses and training and we got along wonderful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much difference in your social life in the two places when you were?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, of course, yes, there was quite a bit of difference because when you went to a dance in Mowbridge, you know, you were very popular because there weren't that many girls, but when you got to Chicago, why there was all kinds of girls, but we didn't really go and dance too much in Chicago. We went to roller skating rink. So we skated a lot. And in the wintertime, we ice skated and everything. And I never really did dance too much because we just didn't do it. I suppose some of the nurses did, but the bunch that I traveled around with, we liked to roller skate and ice skate and things like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have big facilities for that?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh Lord, I should say they did. Oh my, those, those skating rinks were just really big. Yeah. And of course, in like in when I came to Boville, you know, well, I think there were about three of us girls. And of course there were all kinds of lumberjacks there, you know, while Jeepers nothing to go to a dance, you know, and have eight and 10, 12 dances ahead. Yeah. It was really fun. Really popular. Yeah. I never lacked for fellows. I always had plenty of fellows. I didn't say I would lack for them. Eight and 12 dances ahead. Yeah. You mean they'd ask you and you'd have to keep track in your mind. Well, yeah, but of course the guys, they pretty much kept track too. But sometimes of course they were program dances, but most of the time they weren't. But of course I wasn't always able to go either. Although this friend of mine who I worked with and she also graduated from the same hospital I did in Mobridge, but she graduated a year before I did. Well, then she decided to go to Chicago and take, um, she was only two year girl and I was three years, see, but she decided to go to Cook County and I wanted her to wait until I could go, but she wouldn't do it. She went and took her course six months course. Well, then we were together quite a bit and she came to Boville with me and, um, she really didn't care about dancing. She was older, quite a bit older than I, and she didn't like to dance. So most of the time I got to go unless it was some, you know, emergency or something that I couldn't get away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these dances in Boville?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Usually they'd be far, far more men than women. Oh, yes, I should say. Always a lot of fellows and not very many girls. Of course, if the weather was good, by then the girls came from Elk River, you know, and other places to Boville. But, uh, in the wintertime, it was pretty hard to get around, you know. We did many of the lumberjacks who were more of, not Boville people. I mean, people who were, who men who were living in the camps all the time. They would come to. Young men, you know, not really from around Boville, from other places, you know, all over the country, as far as that goes and came there to work in the woods, you know. But they were nice fellows and, uh, well, you know, when they went out any place, they always looked nice and everything. Oh, when you were working at, uh, at Chicago, uh, I know that the city of Chicago in those years had tremendous numbers of immigrants and, and, you know, the city changed and kept growing and different groups were coming in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, did you see that when you were, when you were there?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, I'll tell you where I was at this hospital was on Washington Boulevard and the nurse's home was on Warren Avenue. We had an awful lot of Jewish people, terrible lot of Jewish people. And of course, I suppose we had other people too, but an awful lot of Jews. And we had, uh, some colored people too, because there were quite a few colored people in that, in that district too. But I do think in probably in those days, people didn't really go to the hospital and didn't like it is now where they have Medicare and all this and that, you know. But in those days, it was really expensive to go to a hospital. And you just didn't go unless you absolutely had to go. Of course, like our baby cases and everything like that, we had a lot of babies and things like that. And of course we had a lot of surgery too. And another thing we had was an awful lot of ulcer patients. Dr. Sippy, who is, I suppose he's been gone many years now. I don't know how long he's been gone, but he was an ulcer doctor and we had practically one floor just of nothing in the world, but his patients. But like I say, people just, you just didn't go to the hospital for every little thing like they do nowadays. Did you ever have contact with immigrants and speaking to them like the Jewish people or the... Well, I suppose we did. I don't remember that it was anything out of the ordinary though, just a regular run of people, you know. But you want to remember now, listen, that's good. That goes back a long time, you know, and I can't remember all that stuff. I did. I remember going to the hospital. I did, you know, because I had contact with too many people and I couldn't remember them all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, like the other night I went to a card party over at Clarkson, this lady said to me, I was talking to one of your patients and I said, one of my patients?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And she said, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, was it a man or woman?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: She said, a woman. And she said, she told me what a good nurse you were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, who in the world was it?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: So she says, well, I can't remember her name, but she pointed her up to me. Sure, she was a woman that I had taken care of in Bogle. She had a bad hand. And she said, she showed me how you fixed your hand up. And I said, oh yeah, Edith has always really felt like that. I really did something wonderful for her because you see, I did things that no other nurse would do because we just didn't have a doctor. And I would do all those things, you know, and really God was good to me because I never got in any kind of a mess. And I used to worry about it a lot too, because I thought, well, now I did that. And maybe I shouldn't have, you know, but I couldn't tell you now what Edith did to her hand. But I know it was quite a while and I took care of it and everything. And I don't remember whether I took any stitches or not. It's in this thumb, I think on the right hand. But it is. It's almost a perfect job. She showed me the other night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I had to go, you know, after she told this woman, I walked over to her then and I said, oh, Edith, you're still bragging about me, aren't you?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: She said, yeah, every chance I get, Lucille. And I said, let me see that thumb a year. So she showed it to me and I said, well, it doesn't look like a pretty good job. She said, well, I'd say so. I'd say it was a real good job. But, you know, and then another thing you want to remember that it was hard to work in a place like Beaubil too, because they didn't have a doctor and they thought just because you were a nurse that you should know as much as a doctor. And if you didn't know as much as a doctor, you just weren't very smart. Now I'm telling you. Because they just expected as much of you as they did as a doctor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time period was this when there wasn't any doctor there in Beaubil?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, good God, there was a lot of times that we didn't have a doctor. In fact, practically from the time my kids were growing up, uh, when my daughter was born, that was 26 and, um, we didn't have a doctor then. I had to go to potlatch when she was born and I was gone from home pretty near a month waiting for her. And then after she was born and then when my son, my youngest son was born, he was a nurse. And he had a doctor from Chicago and his wife, who was a nurse and they were a fine team. So I got to stay home for that delivery. I told my husband, I said, I'm just not going to go to the hospital because I said, I'm not going to leave those two kids. I had girl and the boy and I said, I'm just not going to go. And he said, oh, you better go. Because he said, you'll be so particular for everything isn't done right around the house. He said, worry you to death. And I said, no, I'm not going to go. I'm going to have doctor. Now, I don't know for sure what his name was. I don't know if it's really as to what it was, but anyway, he was some kind of a foreigner, I think of some sort. But they came to the house and baby was delivered. And then off and on, we did have doctors, but there was an awful lot of the time that we didn't have any doctors. And Vovil has been without a doctor now for many, many years. You know, I don't know if they even have a nurse up there anymore. I don't know. I don't know. I don't think so. All these years that there was no doctor there, that you were first date nurse for many, many years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was where were you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I'm wondering is how was it set up? Were you the people supposed to go down to potlatch if there was anything at all?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, sure, they had to go if they had to have surgery and things like that. But I used to take care of a lot of them. I only delivered one baby. I was fortunate not to get myself in only one mess. And I come through that all right. But I tell you, I worried myself sick for fear. Everything wouldn't go all right. But it did. And finally, the doctor got there before I had to cut the cord. And oh, Lord, I thought I know how to do it and everything. But I don't know if I can do it or not.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I just was so worried because I thought, oh, my land, what if anything would happen to that baby or that mother?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I'd never forgive myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, was it an unexpected delivery?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: No, but she was a little bit early. And when they called me, she was her labor was so far advanced. And I called the doctor right away. Well, you know, we had to come from Moscow, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, now you're going to come right away?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And he said, yes, I'm going to come right away. Because I said, I'm really worried about cutting that cord. And he said, no, he said, really, it should be about 45 minutes before you cut it anyway. He said, so don't worry about that. And so but nevertheless, I didn't help matters. And I did. I really worried. But there probably are some people around Boville that would tell you. And I'm not saying this to be smart or anything like that, because like I say, I did do a lot of things and I had awfully good luck with anything that I did. And they would tell you that really that I was really an awfully good nurse because I did things for them, you know, and I didn't charge them for them because nobody had any money. And I did all this gratis, you know. And when I did get paid, and of course, when we got this one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the guy working that that?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Yeah, the superintendent of the potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said you're going to get paid for it?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I did. Well, I did after he came, but I didn't. And he said, I want to I had to buy my own supplies and everything. And he said, I want to want you to give me a list of everything that you need.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, well, now, listen, is this going to be just for the people in Boville?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Or I mean, to work for the potlatch, you're going to be for everybody in Boville. And he said, no, he said, putting everybody in Boville is potlatch anyway. And he said, no, I want you to give me a list and we'll get everything that you need. And then he said, anytime you come down and take care of or when they stop by and you give a shot or anything like that, the ambulance stops at your house. I want you to be paid for it. He said, you work for nothing long enough. And he said, from here on out, you're not working for nothing anymore. But all the time I was married for well, I was married in twenty one and we left Boville in fifty three and I did all the nursing in that town from then until I left. Incredible.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Of course, I did have a doc quite there. He was a veterinarian, but he had taken medicine and he didn't tend to be a doctor, but he decided it was going to be too long. So then he decided to be a veterinarian and he was good at that. But he was also pretty good, MD, too. But he would help me out and he would say, well, now I'd say, well, you do it. And he'd say, no, I can't because he said they could come back on me. But he said they couldn't come back on you. So he said, you don't have to be afraid to do it. I'll tell you what to do. And he said, you do it. So that's the way it was. But I sure liked Kenny White. He was wonderful to me. And then another thing I really had a good thing established with the doctors in Moscow. They knew I was reliable and they always let me have narcotics, you know, because of course, in a bad emergency or anything like that, you had to have morphine and things like that. So I never had any trouble having that. And I got along well with all the doctors in Moscow. You know, I worked with all of them because, you know, everybody didn't have the same doctor in Bovil. And one would have this one and one would have the other one. And then they'd say if they needed shops or anything, well, just take this up and give it to Miss Denovin. She'll do it for you, you know. And I gave a lot of penicillin and stuff like that. Old people that had allergies and stuff like that, you know, I gave them shops and everything. I'd like to have a nickel for every hypo I've given. Be a rich woman. You really did that. It sounds like you almost did that at an expense to yourself if you were actually having to put in supplies. Yeah. Well, and then another thing, of course, in the meantime, I was raising my family and helping my husband too. And, well, I was just busy all the time. Sure. Yeah, just all the time. But I never felt like I had to really neglect my family because I couldn't go and take care of people because I had all I could do to go at home. But in the case of emergency, I always did go and help out. In fact, one time I went to help a friend of mine. Her son was having convulsions. And I had sent my daughter home. We just lived little ways. And Helen was just a little girl. And I told her, I said, now, Helen, you go home and pick up some chips for mother. And then when I come home, I can build a fire.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what she did?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: She went out there in the woodshed and grabbed up the axe. She was going to make some kindling. And she was probably, I don't know, probably eight years old. And cut her finger clear through right through there. She still carries a scar. Here was her mother taking care of somebody else. And so then she'd come over. And she was crying, of course, and blood all over everything. And Louise didn't dare tell me because she was afraid it'd upset me so bad. And of course, her son was so bad. And the doctor was there and everything, you know. But she came in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, what's going on out there, in a way? I said, who's that crying?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I said, sounds like Helen. And she said, it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, what's the matter with her?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, she said she'd cut her finger a little bit. But she didn't dare tell me how much she'd cut it. But, you know, it was things that you just had to do, that's all. And I just automatically had to do them. I don't think I ever turned anybody down that came to me and needed help.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And lots of times I'd take care of people maybe two and three weeks. You know, I know one man cut his hand off a bed trying to chop a pesto log and cut his hand real bad. I think I probably took care of his bad hand of history probably two or three weeks. I never got any money out of it. Lots of times I never even got to thank you out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you worked with Doc White, or the vet, what would he...

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: You said you would actually do the work, but he would sometimes give you suggestions on how to do it. Like on what would you need to see... Well, one time there was a real good friend of mine. She came and she had cut her finger real bad. And the darn thing wouldn't heal. And it wouldn't heal, it wouldn't heal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I called Doc White and I said, could you come over?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I said, I got... well, I called her by name, Louise McDonald here, and I said, she's got a bad finger and it won't seem to heal. And she had cut it like this, you know. And there was quite a piece of skin on the back. And I just smacked it up like that, you know, and thought it would heal all right. But it didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, well, do you know what's the matter?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And I said, no. He said, you're going to have to cut that piece of skin off. Because he said, the blood supply is gone. Oh, I said, I couldn't do that. And I said, and at that time, I don't think I had morphine like I did later on. And I said, I wouldn't have a thing to give her. And she said, oh, I can stand it. She said, I can't afford to go to the doctor. And she said, I can stand it. So I sterilized my scissors and cut it off. And the darn thing healed up and filled in just fine. But that's what he told me. See, I didn't know that. And he said, you're going to have to cut that chunk of skin off. And I wanted him to do it, but he wouldn't do it. And then another time, I had all this funny religion people. They were from Elk River. And they brought this girl. And her hand was cut off a little bad. And oh, Lord, I knew she'd have to have a lot of stitches. And I said, well, the only thing that I can do for you is just give you first aid. And I said, you're going to have to take this girl to the doctor. No, they wouldn't take her to the doctor. And I said, well, she's going to have to have those fingers fixed. That's all there is to it. Because, and then, you know, they told me that they belonged to this religion. They didn't believe in doctors. And I said, well, I don't know what to tell you, because I said, there's nothing I can do but wrap up the girl's hand. And that's what I did, wrap it up. Because, like I say, I always had bandages and stuff like that. And I don't know what they did with her. I really don't know whether they took her on home to Elk River or whether they did decide to take her to a doctor. But I said, she's going to have to have stitches in that hand because if you don't, it just won't heal. That's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wonder what, do you remember what religious group that was?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, I don't know. It seemed to me like it was Rose of something or some kind of a funny religion. I don't know. A real unusual one. Yeah. Nothing that you really hear about too often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how is it, again, that you decided to come to Bowville in the first place?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, because I got this job offered to me, see. Dr. Buehler, who was from Seattle and was the head of the Milwaukee Hospitals, he came to Chicago and he was looking for a nurse. In fact, he was looking for two nurses to go to Bowville. And so they told him, or he knew about it or something. I don't know. Maybe Dr. Gibson told him. Maybe he knew about it. I don't know how he knew about it. But anyway, Dr. Buehler talked to me and asked me if I'd like to go to Bowville and work well. Of course, I thought, gee, it would be wonderful to have a job, you know. I guess I thought I wouldn't get one. And so I told him, yeah. And then when I got on my way going to, coming out to Idaho, I stopped in Mobridge to see my sister and her husband. And they both had a fit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They said, well, why didn't you come back to Mobridge?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: You know, there's all kinds of work here for you. There were two hospitals there in Mobridge. And oh, I said, I don't know. I kind of wanted to go out west and see what it was like. And I said, I'll work long enough to make enough money to come home, and then I'll come home. But I didn't do it. They weren't very happy about it either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This doctor was a Seattle doctor?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Ahaan Dr. Buehler, who was head of the hospitals. And he was a Milwaukee Hospital Association. And of course, this hospital at Mobridge. And then there was one at Three Forks, Montana.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And then there was one at Orfino. And then of course, we were affiliated with the hospital in Chicago. That's how we got to take our last six months there, which was really nice coming from such a small hospital, you know. But I don't know how they found out for me. That's too long ago, I can't remember. But I know that I came out here and worked in Bowville, in a way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what happened when you first got out to Bowville and saw what it was like?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, I don't know. I just fell in, you know. When you go to Rome, you have to do the Romans, too. We didn't have any lights at night, you know. We were on a call at night and everything, you know. Worked all day, and then at night we were on a call. Of course, we had the patients pretty well. Of course, if there was somebody real sick, it was different. But most of the patients didn't need anything at night. And if their patients were real sick, maybe we could have some of their folks stay with them. And then they would call us if they needed us. Maybe they needed a shot or something like that, then we'd have to get off our cars and do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the hospital in Bowville like then? What were the hospital facilities? What did they have?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, they had everything. You know where that hospital is. It's Hall's Apartments, you know. That was our hospital. And on the—well, as you go into the hospital, on your left, on the second floor, was our operating room. We had a real nice operating room. We had a sterilizing room and everything. And the doctor had his office downstairs, you know, and he had a little place off where he had all his medicine, you know. And he would dispense that medicine. People needed it. And of course, we also had a pharmacist there in Bowville at that time, too. You know, at one time, Bowville was quite a town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: We would assist doctor just like a doctor would, you know, because we had to. And we got along fine. I can't think of anybody ever dying from neglect or anything like that. We had one man die, and I think that Dr. Gibson waited too long to operate on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he died on the operating table, but who am I to say that?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Because he was a very conscientious man, and he was a doctor. By golly, when he diagnosed a case, you knew what was wrong with that patient. And when he went in, he found out what he said it was to. That's a damn sight more than the doctors know nowadays. They guess at an awful lot, and they have so much better facilities and everything. And that's what I can't understand. Why these doctors don't know what's wrong with these people anymore. Now, my son, they doctored him for two years for ulcers. And I never did think he had ulcers. I thought that he either had gallstones or he had kidney stones. Well, he finally got so sick, and so his doctor called in another doctor, and they did an exploratory on him. And Jack had all the symptoms of gallbladder, and I couldn't see why that doctor couldn't see it. He was so jaundiced and everything, but they said that the x -rays didn't show any gallstones, because he didn't have stones. He had just the gravel, which was in the gall duct, causing that terrific pain, you know. And the sweeper just poured off him. Well, he did this exploratory, and they found out that he had this gravel in the gallbladder duct. So, of course, they removed his gallbladder, and he felt fine. So I said to this Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Barnes, who is a surgeon, and that's about all he does, I said, did you find any evidence of ulcers?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And he said no. But he said, you know, you don't get gallbladder overnight either, you know. But I couldn't understand it, because I knew that that boy either had to have gallbladder or he had to have kidney stones, one of the two, because he had this terrific pain, and he had all the symptoms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, if I would know that, why wouldn't those doctors know that?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And another case was with my husband.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was in the hospital, and he was very sick, and I said to his doctor, I said, did you take an x -ray of his chest?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And he said no. And I said, well, I don't know, but I think he's probably got emphysema. And he said, well, okay, we'll take an x -ray of his chest then. So they did. And he showed Merle the x -ray. And he told Merle that there was a small area in his lungs, see. So then the next time Merle went to the doctor, he wasn't feeling very good, so I thought, well, I better go along with him in case something happens. So I went to him with him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said to me, do you want to see that x -ray of your husband's lungs?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And he didn't know that I was a nurse, because Merle said he never told him, and I certainly didn't tell him I was a nurse. And I said, well, I probably wouldn't know anything about it. And he said, well, I'll show it to you. And I said, well, I thought you told my husband that it was just a small area. I said, it looks to me like that's a pretty big area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, he looked at me so funny, he said, do you suppose that man could have pneumonia?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And I said, well, I wouldn't know. So he put him on antibiotics, and he did. He got better. And he said, now, I can only leave you on that for a week. And then Merle went right back into a slump again. So then he had to give him to him for, I don't know, for a week, but a few days anyway. Now, there was that nut.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said to my doctor in Moscow, I said, shouldn't have that doctor known that that man had pneumonia when he took that x -ray?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And of course, you know, a doctor would never run the other guy down. And besides my doctor and this doctor were very good friends anyway. In fact, he'd recommended this doctor down here. Well, he said, I would think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you, when you, Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gibson, was he, you feel that he was better, better than that, better than most doctors you've done?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: You're damn right, I figure. I figure that he was a lot better than the average doctor nowadays. No, I mean, I mean, in those days. Oh, yes. He was a brilliant man. He really was. He was a brilliant man. He knew exactly what he was doing all the time. And another thing, he had his internship at Cook County in New York, in Chicago, which you really got to be right up there amongst the best of them to do your intern at Cook County. Now, I'm telling you, because you get everything imaginable there, you know. And his wife was a nurse. And of course, that helped him too, you know. She used to help us. She used to come and give the anesthetics. And one time she was pregnant and she gave these anesthetics all the time, I guess, while she was pregnant. And she had an awful time with this baby. He just simply didn't get along. In fact, I think he died before he was too old. And I don't know. I couldn't prove it, but that's what they said because she gave that and gave those anesthetics, you know, and she shouldn't have given them to him. But I don't know whether there's any truth to that or not. I really don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I had heard a couple of people talk about Dr.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Gibson in a mixed way, saying he was a good doctor, but then also that he could be rather touchy with the patients and that he was... He was an awful, cranky thing. Yes, he was. And lots of times he wasn't very nice to his patients. That's right. Yeah, that's the truth. Yeah, that's the truth. And you know, Dr. Gibson, when he died, and I had this man tell me, and it was nobody in the world but Dr. Dupree from Palouse, and he told me that Dr. Gibson told him, he said, you know, I'm going to have a terrible, terrible lot to account for. And he said, really, I'm afraid to die. And Dr. Dupree said to him, well, he said, I think that you better call in your minister and talk to him. And he had a terrible horror of cancer, and that's what he died with, was cancer. But he was, he was pretty gruff and everything, and he was a man that you really had to put him in his place. You really did. I told him one time to go to hell. I said, I don't know, Dr. Gibson, we just can't please you. I said, when the hospital is full, you're so mean and ornery, and when it isn't full, you're mean and ornery, and we just don't know how to please you. That's all there is to it. We just don't know what to do. And I said, as far as I'm concerned, you can go to hell, because I said, I have worked myself to death since I've been here, and I don't have to work for you. There's other places that I can go. And I said, I'm going to. He gave me the devil because this woman, who had had a gallbladder operation, her folks came and they it was probably a week after she'd been operated on, but she wasn't a very young woman, older woman, and they brought her some. I don't know what kind of pie it was. I believe it was banana cream pie or something, but they didn't give her very much, but it did make her awful sick. Well, then Dr. Gibson told me I should have known better than that. I said, I didn't know had I known that they brought that pie. I wouldn't have let him give it to her, but I didn't know. And I said, we can't watch the people all the time because I said, we have too much to do. So then he came down kind of off his high horse. And I said, you expect so much of us, doctor. And I said, it isn't humanly possible to do all that we do and all that we should do. You can't do it because there isn't time enough in the days to do it. But and then I did. I told him I was going to leave and I did leave, too. I went back to Chicago again. That's when I went to Contagious and worked. And they want me to stay there, too. But I was kind of one of these people. I didn't know what I wanted to do. I wanted to get around and see things. Then I went back to Spokane. I worked in Spokane, Sacred Heart. I was in charge of Edgecliffe for a year and a half. And not the whole Edgecliffe, just the men's floor at Edgecliffe. I had 36 men on my floor and only five ambulatory patients. And I had one orderly and one nurse's aide to help me. So you can imagine how we work to take care of those people. In the morning they had to have stuff either eggnog or some cockeyed thing. And in the afternoon they had to. And I just worked myself to death there. And finally Dr. Gibson came to see me. He wanted me to come back to Bowville. And when he saw me he had a fit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, how much weight have you lost?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And I said, well, quite a bit. And I said, not only that, I said I've developed a cough, too. I said, I don't know whether it's a sympathetic cough or it really is a TB cough. And he said, well, I'm telling you one thing. You better get out of here while you're able to get out. So I left Edgecliffe then and I came back to Bowville and worked for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy, you talked about working at an Edgecliffe.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: It sounds extremely rugged compared to these other places that you talked about working. Well, of course there were an awful lot of them that weren't awfully sick. But there were a lot of them that were sick and needed a lot of care and everything. And another thing, it was so hard to keep other charts because they were on four hour temp, you know, and all that, you know. You had to take your temperatures every four hours. There was so much to it, you know, so much book work and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gosh, I look back now and think, how in the name of gosh did I do that?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And that's what I'm telling you. All of the nurses, not only me, but every nurse worked hard then. And we just went along with it. We knew we had to work hard. We had to work. That's all there was to it, to get done and do what they expected us to do. Of course, they were awful good to their nurses. We got wonderful food there. We had a wonderful place to stay at Edgecliffe. And everybody was lovely to us. When I was there, Dr. Miller was there. And our superintendent of nurses was Mrs. ... no, it wasn't Mrs. Miss Hahn. And she's probably been dead for years, I don't know. But I don't know whether Dr. Miller is still alive or not. I really don't know. He was a pretty young man when I was there. And so he probably is still alive, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the course of tuberculosis like then, the disease?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Now there is a little of it. And then it seemed to be so prevalent. I've heard so many people locally talk about it. So many people died with TB. Well, of course, they had to be awful careful with their sputum. And we ... I can't remember whether we had Kleenex there or what we had for them. I don't know, maybe we had little squares of gauze for them. I don't remember. That's too long ago. I can't remember about that. But I remember that everybody had a sack on their bed. And these were ... we didn't handle them. The patients themselves had to put those little squares, or whatever we used, in those paper sacks. And then, of course, everything was burned up. We had a man that came along and collected those sacks and everything. And then we would put Kleenex on their beds, you know. And so we didn't contaminate ourselves. And we were really careful when we were taking care of real, real sick patients. Why we wore masks and things, you know. So that they wouldn't be coughing in our faces and so forth, you know. But they were a nice bunch of ... I always rather take care of men than women. I like to take care of men. It seemed like I got along with them better. And, you know, with women, there's always something wrong with them, you know. And they're hard to please and everything. But I always preferred to take care of men. That's why. I could have been in the children's ward, but I said no. I thought I'd go along with the men. And they said, well, they would rather that I would, because it was easier to get a nurse that wanted to deal with the children, you know. And I said no. I thought I would be happier taking care of them. And I was. And, like I say, I had a real nice orderly. And my nurse was very good. And they were just like everybody else. They worked hard, too. And the three of us did manage it. But I don't know how we did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there really any hope for most of the people who had tuberculosis?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, yeah. So indeed there was, I'm sure. We lost them, too. But not too many deaths. Because, you know, they were taking good care of it. They got good food and everything, you know. And got good care. And that's what all it took, you know. And then lots of times we'd push them from their rooms out into these porches, you know. It was cold in the devil, you know. And believed in fresh air, you know. And then they'd be out so long. And then they'd have to be pulled back in, you know. But, of course, when they were hemorrhaging, everybody rushed in. And it was really an emergency. And, in fact, in the afternoons, when we had our time off from two to, I can't remember, it was two to four, probably not. It was probably one to three that we had off. Why then they had an emergency bell. And there were always men, you know, these ambulatory patients that could let us know. They'd ring the emergency bell, you know. And, of course, everybody'd fly over there then, you know. And, of course, then the doctor'd come and take care of them when they were having these hemorrhages, you know. I can't remember now what we did for them. I don't remember. It's too long ago. Too many other things happened to me. But, like I say, I enjoyed my work there, too. But I did. I got awful thin. And I developed this cough, and I don't know whether it was strictly bad cough, and there was danger of me getting TB, or whether it was just a sympathetic cough. And I was just coughing because everybody else did, maybe. I don't know. But I, like I say, I never did any nursing that I didn't really like it. I just loved it, every bit of it. No matter how dirty or how nice it was, it seemed like that I just fell into it. Just wonderful.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, like all this weight that you lost up there doing that work, did you realize that you were...

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, I knew that I was working terribly hard, but I thought, well, work won't hurt anybody. If you're born to bear the load, work isn't going to hurt you. But it did. And, like I say, not long ago, I had a little bit of trouble with my heart. And I said, well, I've always been a person that's taken good care of myself. I said, I've never drank, I've never smoked. And I said, I can't see why there'd be anything wrong with my heart. And they said, the only thing that's wrong with your heart is that you always had worked so hard. That's probably what's done it. And that could be the only thing because I haven't... But my heart's all right. My doctor wasn't worried about it. But he said, it was missing a beat, and that they would have to get it regulated. So then I went to another doctor because I got poisoned and was awfully sick last summer, and was sick for a month. And my daughter insisted I go to somebody else, so I did. And he said, well, yeah, your heart is missing a beat. But he said, that's all right, he said. And he said, I'm not going to give you anything for it. He said, it'll be all right. He said, well, it'll work itself out all right. But he said, I think you should slow down a little bit. But, you know, I was married... Well, we had our 50th wedding anniversary in 71, and my husband passed away 17 months ago. And so, you see, I was married quite a while. And I was never sick. I was, you know, always felt wonderful and everything. Always worked like a horse from the time I was married. And of course, I worked like a horse before I was married, you. But I don't think that hard work hurts anybody. If you've got a strong enough back, I really don't think so. It never hurt me, but like I say, I always took pretty good care of myself, too. As good care as I could. When I was working at Edgecliff, I realized that I was probably working too hard there, but I thought, well, yeah, I don't think it'll hurt me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds as though that your attitude about work...

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: You weren't in it to make a living, but you didn't expect to make... You weren't just trying to get the best paying possible. No, uh -uh. No, that never... I always wondered if maybe I was earning the money that I was getting. I always felt like maybe I wasn't, you know. And Jeepers, when we left... See, we had the Laytown Nursing Home when it was first built. And like I say, I wouldn't have been any more surprised if they'd have come and asked me if I wanted to be the mayor of Moscow. I wouldn't have been a bit more surprised. But all the time we were there, I worked hard, too. And my husband and I only got $6 .50 a month, the two of us. And then they brought these other nuts in, and they got $1 ,000 a month and didn't do a damn thing. So that's gratitude for you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you decided to go back to Beauville anyway, despite the fact that I had taken you and Mr ....

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And Dr. Gibson hadn't left the best of friends, but... No, but Dr. Gibson, like I say, I'm not saying this to be smart or anything, but Dr. Gibson realized after I was gone, but a good nurse I was. And he wanted to get me back if he could. And when he came to Spokane and knew that I was at Edgecliffe, and the superintendent of nurses at Sacred Heart recommended me for the job out of Edgecliffe, she said, I think it would be wonderful for you, Mr. Dell. And I said, well, I don't know if I could handle it or not. She said, sure, you could handle it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why couldn't you handle it?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: And I said, well, I don't know. I said, I'm a little bit afraid. And she said, no, you can do it. She said, and you do it too. She said, I hate to lose you, but she said, it's a good job for you. She said, it'll be good for you. And so I went out there and I had no trouble getting the job at all. Of course, like I say, she had recommended me. But... And like when we were recommended for this job in Moscow, people that we didn't really know too well recommended us for this job. And I am sure if you ask anybody in Moscow that while we were there for six years, that there was never any talk about the nursing home at all. There couldn't have been because everybody did their job. And we had no turnover of help. We had girls work for there. Came the first day we were there and were there when we left. And we just didn't have any turnover of help at all. Everybody enjoyed their work and wanted to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were in Sacred Heart, did you have any...

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: That was... Now, Spokane was an altogether different city than Chicago. Oh, Lord, yes. Spokane was like a town. Town. Well, when I was at Sacred Heart, the only thing I did there was special. Just special cases, you know, like operatives and things like that. I didn't work, you know, general duty or anything like that. I just special when I was there. And I lived about two blocks in the hospital, so it was real nice for me. But I didn't work there too long because this job came up and Sister Bernice recommended me for it. You must have liked Bow Hill pretty well to have been willing to go back there. Oh, yeah, I guess so. And, of course, I'm one of these kind of people. I forget pretty quick, too, you know. People can hurt me, but I don't hold a grudge, you know, and I forget. And, of course, like I say, you know, we just never said anything. Doctors like that. And I was really surprised at myself when I came out and told Dr. Gibson he could go to hell. Well, when you went back and worked for him again, were you still in a pretty... Oh, yeah, yeah, he knew that I wasn't going to take anything off him because I gave him to understand that the only way I'd come back and work for him was if I didn't have to work myself to death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, if I'm working myself to death here, why?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I said, coming and working for you isn't going to help matters any. But, you know, if the attitude around there is good, why then is your work is easy. And Dr. Gibson, he... But, of course, he's quite a bit like me, too, you know. He'd get mad at things if everything didn't go just so, you know. And he was just a... Oh, a person that was very meticulous about everything, you know. Everything had to be just so. Dr. Gibson would never have an infection because he was just such a clean person, and that hospital had to be so clean and everything, you know. And why, staff infection or anything like that, why that would have been the last thing we'd ever thought of. Because we kept things clean, we had to. And Dr. Gibson, when you went to work for him, he told you, he said, I'll tell you once. And he said, I might tell you the second time, but I'm not going to tell you the third time what I expect of you. He said, you're going to know by that time. And we sure did know by that time, too. But, of course, I'm pretty much like that myself. And things have got to be just so, or I just can't stand it. That's all there is to it. And this other nurse, you know, like we had a cook working for us, and she said to me one day, I want to ask you something, Mr. Dalin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, okay, what do you want to ask me?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: She said, I want to ask you why in the world you always look like you just stepped out of a band box. And Miss Hennessy, her uniform was always dirty, and her apron's dirty and everything. And she said, you both do the same work. And I said, it's just because we're different people. And I can work, and I wouldn't get dirty, you know. But she just seemed like she couldn't do anything unless she was all messed up, see. And that was the way Dr. Gibson was. Everything in the surgery had to be just so, and if it wasn't just so, why you were told about it. And he said, I expect my instruments to be kept just so, because in the case of emergency, he said, they just simply have to be kept.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know when I talk to the old -time loggers, just about every one of them remembers some bad, really bad accidents because of the work being so dangerous. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many of those cases wind up in Boville for first aid on their way?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, yes, like, you know, arms off and legs off and stuff like that, you know. Now, Merle worked with a man that got his arm off, but I don't think we had a hospital in Boville when he got hurt. But I remember giving him a shot and everything and sending him down to Potlatch. Yeah, we had bad accidents, you know. But, oh, I don't know, I can't remember those really, those really awful bad accidents. And if we did have them, like I say, they got along all right. Of course, I suppose we lost some of them, you know. But not too many, I don't think. And this man that died on the operating table, I always will think that Dr. Gibson neglected him. I don't know why he did it, because he wasn't a man like that, but I felt like that if he would have operated on him in the morning instead of waiting until at night to do it, that he would have saved the man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was wrong?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, he'd been hit by a log or something, and I think he was injured internally, you know, probably bleeding, you know. But I can't remember all those things. I just can't remember them, because, like I say, there's been too many things happen in my life. And personally, I've done too many things myself, you know, that I just can't remember all those things. I just can't. I couldn't help you out too much on those bad accidents. But as I remember, we didn't lose too many patients there. And Dr. did really advanced surgery, too. Did a lot of hysterectomies, gallbladders, and appendix, and, you know, pus appendix cases, why it just seems like they got along just marvelous for Dr. Gibson. Because I think his technique was so good that there just wasn't any danger or anything, you know. And like I say, he worked with us nurses. He didn't have another doctor to assist him, you know. He had to do it all himself. No, I think, I don't care. Even if Dr. Gibson and I had trouble in everything, I never would say that he wasn't a wonderful doctor, because he was. He was a wonderful doctor. Yeah, he really was. And I think a lot more than I do now, the doctors, because I don't know. I think these doctors nowadays, let a lot of people die that they don't know what's wrong with them, and I can't understand it. I just can't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me, as I remember, that there was another doctor who was, in a way, at least I think the man died. He had a ruptured appendix.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: There was another one of the doctors in Bowville, and this guy was on ether. Yeah, uh -huh. That was Dr. Visser. And, yeah, I told his wife, I said, if you don't send your husband down to potlatch, he's going to die. And I said, now for heaven's sake, don't let Dr. Visser hear that, or tell him anything about it. So then they just told him that they decided that they wanted to send him down to Dr. Gibson. And he did die. That was Frank Mosher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, is that when you, had you tried to get him to look at the guy and he'd been out?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Of course, that was after I was married, too. And I was up at the hospital taking care of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that he'd been on ether until then?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: No, I didn't. No, I didn't know it, because I wasn't up there. I didn't know anything about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you actually weren't working in there?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Oh, no. No, no. I'd just help out, you know, when they get busy at the hospital, or maybe some real sick patient or something, and I'd help out. And then I did. I went down to potlatch and took care of this man, too, but didn't take care of him very long, because he didn't live. And his wife is still in Lewiston. I see her once in a while, not too often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you reach your husband?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I met him in Bowville. I always said I was going to marry a farmer, because I wanted to live on a farm. I love farms. But I guess I should have stayed in Minnesota, because of course there wasn't too many farmers out there where I was, in a way. I'm still looking for my farmer, by the way, if you know rich farmers. I don't think I could do very good on a farm anymore, though, because I just can't work like I used to work. That's all there is to it. I just can't do it. Of course, I can put in pretty good days work, too, but... Of course, I don't know. The farms are so modern now, I guess they don't have to work very hard out there. Well, what was Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dunner been doing?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: He was a lumberjack. And then after he'd been a lumberjack for years, while he went in the store up at Bowville, we were in the store for 18 years.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: It's too bad that Merle couldn't have had an education. He was a smart man, and he should have gone to college. He could do about anything. He was a pretty good carpenter, and he was a good businessman. When he was at Blatetown Nursing Home, he really did a good job out there, too. Good with books and figures and things like that, you know. Run it very economical. Of course, he knew how to buy. Having been in a store, he knew how to buy. And that was one reason why we got the job, was because I was a nurse, and Merle had been in business, and they knew that he would know how to buy, and he certainly did. And they got that kid in, just because he was a college graduate and he didn't know anything. And he left him $50 ,000 in debt when he left. But they probably thought more of him than they did of Merle. I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I doubt they could, in looking back at it.

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: Well, like I say, I don't think that you would find anybody in Moscow that would say that we didn't do a good job when we were out there. There was never any trouble or anything. And the commissioners, we got along wonderful with them, and they all liked us, and the doctors liked us. It was just because we didn't belong to the wrong party. We were strong Democrats, and old Smiley was a Republican, and he didn't like us, and his old sister, she looked like an old sport instead of a nurse, and she didn't like us. I hope you're not recording all this. I couldn't prove that she was. I said she looked like one. She might not have been one, but she looked like one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When, let's see, there were things I was going to ask you about being in Bow Hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that thing?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, there were some times that you mentioned to me, other times about when you were doing the nursing yourself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One time you mentioned, was it a guy's lip was cut pretty bad? What was the story of what happened to him?

speaker: Lucille Riddell Denevan
words: I don't know. I showed his lip up, and I thought I'd probably lose my job, but Dr. Gibson told me he couldn't done any better himself. But you know, I had an unlimited amount of nothing in the world but guts. That's the only thing that you could say. I could no more do those things now than I could fly to Guinea, but it just seemed like I don't know. I didn't want to. I tried to tell that man that I couldn't do it, but he wouldn't take no for an answer. Aunt Hennessy, she was egging me on, she said, sew it up for him as long as he wants it sewed up. And I said, oh no, I'm afraid Dr. Gibson would can me. No, he said he won't can you. And I said, well, he's going to be back tomorrow. And I said, maybe if I don't put the stitches in right, maybe he can take them out and fix it. We'll put you to sleep and fix you up. So I said, now you'll be sure to come in tomorrow. So he did. And like I say, I sewed up legs and things like that, you know, no more shit of dentists than anything. But I don't know. I just thought I could do anything, and I did it. And like I say, the Lord was good to me, and I never had any trouble. Of course, I was so darn clean with everything. I couldn't have any infections, that's for sure. But then I didn't always think that maybe I did the right thing, you know. I'd worry all night about it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
